perturbation.original_text: 5) If Ultragenyx obtains regulatory approval for the processed Product, the Parties shall enter into a separate commercial supply agreement for the Product that sets forth the forecasting and ordering mechanism for commercial supply of the Product, enablement of the manufacturing process in the event of a failure to supply, the term of such commercial supply agreement and other customary terms and conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: 5) If Ultragenyx obtains regulatory approval for the processed Product, the Parties will negotiate a supply agreement, but Cremer is not obligated to enter one.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to enter a commercial supply agreement after regulatory approval introduces uncertainty and potential non-compliance, especially if Ultragenyx relies on Cremer's supply to meet regulatory requirements and market demand. This change could introduce a breach of good faith and fair dealing, especially if Ultragenyx invested in reliance on the prior agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the implied obligation of good faith and fair dealing under the UCC, particularly section 2-306, which addresses output and requirements contracts. While the initial agreement is not strictly an output or requirements contract, the exclusive supply arrangement and the anticipation of a commercial supply agreement after regulatory approval imply a duty of good faith in negotiating that subsequent agreement. Removing the obligation entirely could be seen as a breach of that duty, especially if Ultragenyx has invested significantly in reliance on Cremer's continued supply.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-306. Output, Requirements and Exclusive Dealings. (1) A term which measures the quantity by the output of the seller or the requirements of the buyer means such actual output or requirements as may occur in good faith , except that no quantity unreasonably disproportionate to any stated estimate or in the absence of a stated estimate to any normal or otherwise comparable prior output or requirements may be tendered or demanded. (2) A lawful agreement by either the seller or the buyer for exclusive dealing in the kind of goods concerned imposes unless otherwise agreed an obligation by the seller to use best efforts to supply the goods and by the buyer to use best efforts to promote their sale . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the obligation for the parties to enter into a commercial supply agreement after regulatory approval, replacing it with a discretionary negotiation. This contradicts the implied obligation of good faith and fair dealing, particularly under UCC 2-306, as the original agreement implies a duty of good faith in negotiating that subsequent agreement, especially if Ultragenyx has invested significantly based on Cremer's supply. Scraped Law Snippet 1 emphasizes 'good faith' in output and requirements contracts, which this change potentially violates.

perturbation.original_text: 2) If a Party materially breaches an obligation under this Agreement and does not cure such breach within sixty (60) days of receiving notice of such breach from the non-breaching Party, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the breaching Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 2) If a Party materially breaches an obligation under this Agreement, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the breaching Party, regardless of any cure period.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the cure period for a material breach contradicts standard contract law principles that favor allowing the breaching party an opportunity to remedy the situation. This might run afoul of good faith and fair dealing requirements, and specific statutes in certain jurisdictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 3275. Relief in Case of Forfeiture.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under California Civil Code § 3275, relief from forfeiture is often granted if a party is willing to compensate the other party for any damages caused by the breach. Eliminating the cure period effectively creates a forfeiture situation, and a court might be unwilling to enforce such a clause without allowing the breaching party an opportunity to remedy the breach and avoid termination.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3275. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. RELIEF IN GENERAL [3274 - 3275] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 3275. Whenever, by the terms of an obligation, a party thereto incurs a forfeiture, or a loss in the nature of a forfeiture, by reason of his failure to comply with its provisions, he may be relieved therefrom, upon making full compensation to the other party, except in case of a grossly negligent, willful, or fraudulent breach of duty. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the cure period for a material breach, which contradicts the principle in California Civil Code § 3275 that allows relief from forfeiture if the breaching party compensates the other party. Scraped Law Snippet 1 directly quotes CIV 3275 stating that a party incurring a forfeiture due to failure to comply with an obligation may be relieved if they make full compensation. By eliminating the cure period, the contract denies the breaching party this opportunity, creating a contradiction with the law's intent to provide relief from forfeiture.
